## Compact Rules

### fastapi-expert

- Keep route handlers thin; move business logic and pure transformations into helpers/services.
- Validate external inputs explicitly, especially uploaded files and optional filenames.
- Return stable JSON response shapes and document them through FastAPI/OpenAPI.
- Prefer dependency injection for heavyweight resources if startup cost or testability matters.
- Use async handlers carefully: avoid blocking CPU-heavy/audio transcription work on the event loop in production paths.

### python-pro

- Use type hints on public functions and boundaries.
- Prefer explicit error handling over broad exceptions or silent failures.
- Keep I/O, environment reads, and pure logic separated for testability.
- Use standard-library context managers for temporary files/resources.
- Avoid import-time side effects when they make tests slow or brittle; wrap heavyweight initialization when possible.

### python-clean-code

- Use descriptive names that reveal intent; avoid abbreviations outside local, obvious loops.
- Keep functions small and single-purpose; extract logic when a function mixes orchestration and transformation.
- Do not add redundant comments; comments should explain why, not restate what the code says.
- Remove dead code and avoid magic numbers by naming domain constants.
- Leave touched Python code cleaner than found without broad unrelated rewrites.

### python-testing

- Prefer pytest for unit/integration tests when adding a test runner.
- Test pure helpers directly and isolate heavyweight Whisper/model behavior behind mocks or fixtures.
- Cover boundary cases for empty input, malformed segments, short chunks, timing math, and file cleanup.
- Keep tests deterministic and fast; avoid downloading/loading real ML models in unit tests.
- Add coverage tooling (`pytest-cov`) when establishing quality gates.

### api-design

- Use resource-oriented route names and stable HTTP status semantics.
- Return consistent error responses for invalid file type, missing filename, transcription failure, and server errors.
- Document accepted content types and response schemas.
- Avoid leaking internal filesystem paths or stack traces in API responses.
- Keep versioning strategy explicit before public API changes.

### secure-code-guardian

- Treat uploads as untrusted: validate extension/content type, size, and cleanup behavior.
- Never trust `UploadFile.filename` for filesystem paths; use generated temporary paths only.
- Ensure temporary files are deleted on success and failure.
- Avoid returning raw exception details to clients.
- Consider rate limits/timeouts for CPU-heavy transcription endpoints.

### devops-engineer

- CI should install dependencies reproducibly and run tests/lint before deployment.
- Deployment commands must use valid FastAPI/uvicorn invocation semantics.
- Pin or lock dependencies before production deployment where possible.
- Keep secrets out of workflow files; use GitHub secrets/environment variables.
- Separate build/test/deploy stages so quality failures block deploys.

### code-reviewer

- Review correctness, security, performance, maintainability, and tests.
- Flag blocking event-loop work, import-time heavyweight initialization, upload safety gaps, and missing tests.
- Prioritize actionable findings by severity; do not nitpick low-value style issues.
- Verify claims against the actual diff/files.

### test-master

- Identify missing unit, integration, and API contract coverage.
- Prefer mocks for external/model dependencies and sandbox-mode tests for API flows.
- Make quality gates executable with clear commands.
- Report risk when no test runner or CI gate exists.

### cognitive-doc-design

- Lead with the decision/outcome, then provide context.
- Use tables/checklists for capabilities and risks.
- Keep SDD artifacts concise and reviewable.
- Separate facts detected from recommendations.
